article.title: North Korean Hackers Hijack Axios npm Package, Pushing Cross-Platform RAT to Millions of Developers
article.summary: Attackers compromised the lead maintainer's npm account and published two malicious Axios versions that deployed remote access trojans across Windows, macOS, and Linux during a three-hour window.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

Axios, the JavaScript HTTP client downloaded more than 100 million times per week from npm, was compromised in a coordinated supply chain attack on March 31, 2026. The attacker hijacked the npm account of the project's lead maintainer and published two poisoned versions that silently installed a cross-platform remote access trojan on developer machines, CI/CD pipelines, and production systems. Google's Threat Intelligence Group has attributed the attack to a suspected North Korean threat actor, according to [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/31/hacker-hijacks-axios-open-source-project-used-by-millions-to-push-malware/).

The malicious versions were live for approximately three hours before npm's security team removed them, but the scale of Axios's install base means the blast radius could be significant. The incident is the latest in a sustained wave of npm supply chain attacks that has included the [Trivy/CanisterWorm compromise](/article/2026-03/23-trivy-supply-chain-compromise-spawns-canisterworm-the-first-npm-worm-to-use-blockchain-for-command-and-control) earlier this month.

## What Happened

The attacker gained control of the npm account belonging to Jason Saayman, the primary Axios maintainer, and changed the account's registered email address to a Proton Mail address under their control, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The compromise appears to have been carried out using a stolen long-lived npm access token rather than through the project's configured OIDC trusted publishing system, as reported by [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/axios-supply-chain-attack-pushes-cross.html).

The attacker then published `axios@1.14.1` at 00:21 UTC and `axios@0.30.4` at 01:00 UTC, hitting both the current and legacy release branches within 39 minutes. Rather than modifying the Axios source code directly, both versions injected a new dependency called `plain-crypto-js@4.2.1` into the package.json file, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The package had not existed before that day and was never actually imported by the Axios codebase; its sole purpose was to execute a malicious postinstall script.

The malicious dependency had been staged roughly 18 hours in advance: a clean version (`plain-crypto-js@4.2.0`) was published first to establish a publishing history before the weaponized `4.2.1` followed, according to [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/axios-supply-chain-attack-pushes-cross.html). The timing of publication, just after midnight UTC on a Sunday night, was designed to maximize the window before maintainers and npm security staff could respond.

## Technical Details

The postinstall hook executed a dropper script (`setup.js`) that used two layers of obfuscation: reversed Base64 encoding with character substitution and an XOR cipher with the key `OrDeR_7077`, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The dropper detected the host operating system and deployed platform-specific payloads:

- **macOS**: An AppleScript downloaded a binary to `/Library/Caches/com.apple.act.mond`, masquerading as an Apple system daemon, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).
- **Windows**: The malware copied PowerShell to `%PROGRAMDATA%\wt.exe` to evade detection and executed it via a hidden VBScript launcher, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).
- **Linux**: A Python-based payload was retrieved and stored at `/tmp/ld.py`, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).

All three variants delivered a fully functional remote access trojan capable of executing arbitrary commands, retrieving and running additional binaries, enumerating the filesystem, and beaconing to a command-and-control server at `sfrclak[.]com` on port 8000 every 60 seconds, as reported by [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/axios-supply-chain-attack-pushes-cross.html). After deployment, the dropper self-deleted and replaced its modified `package.json` with a clean copy to obstruct forensic analysis.

## Attribution

Google told [TechCrunch](https://techcrunch.com/2026/03/31/hacker-hijacks-axios-open-source-project-used-by-millions-to-push-malware/) that its security researchers are linking the compromise to a suspected North Korean threat actor tracked as UNC1069, a group known for targeting centralized cryptocurrency exchanges, software developers at financial institutions, high-technology companies, and individuals at venture capital funds. The macOS RAT's internal name, `macWebT`, references malware previously associated with BlueNoroff, another North Korean threat group specialized in financially motivated attacks, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).

## Response and Impact

Multiple security firms, including Socket, Aikido, StepSecurity, Huntress, and Snyk, detected and reported the compromise. npm's security team removed both malicious versions approximately three hours after publication, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). The attacker also compromised Saayman's GitHub account and removed security-related reports from project collaborators, as reported by [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).

With approximately 400 million monthly downloads, any environment that resolved to the affected versions during the three-hour exposure window may have executed the malicious payload automatically during `npm install` with no user interaction required, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). Two additional packages, `@shadanai/openclaw` and `@qqbrowser/openclaw-qbot`, were also found distributing the same malware through vendored dependencies, as reported by [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/axios-supply-chain-attack-pushes-cross.html).

Mandiant CTO Charles Carmakal warned that "hundreds of thousands of stolen credentials" have emerged from recent supply chain incidents, potentially triggering cascading compromises, cryptocurrency theft, ransomware, and extortion campaigns, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/).

## What We Don't Know

The exact method by which the attacker obtained the maintainer's npm access token has not been disclosed. Saayman has stated he had two-factor authentication enabled on "practically everything," but the token compromise vector remains under investigation, according to [The Hacker News](https://thehackernews.com/2026/03/axios-supply-chain-attack-pushes-cross.html). The full scope of downstream impact, including how many systems actually installed the poisoned versions during the three-hour window, has not yet been quantified. It also remains unclear whether the stolen credentials from infected machines have already been used in follow-on attacks.

## Remediation

Organizations are advised to audit their dependency lockfiles for `axios@1.14.1`, `axios@0.30.4`, or any version of `plain-crypto-js`. Any system that installed one of these versions should be treated as compromised, according to [BleepingComputer](https://www.bleepingcomputer.com/news/security/hackers-compromise-axios-npm-package-to-drop-cross-platform-malware/). Safe versions to pin include `axios@1.14.0` or `axios@0.30.3`. All credentials, tokens, and CI/CD secrets accessible from affected machines should be rotated immediately.
